body_txt: Panthera Awesome with a white color variant. There's something visually incredible about a large member of the cat family with white fur. Given white often represents purity and innocence , a feline creature with fur can be seen as majestic and divine. Often, these cats have a mystical origin . White tigers and lions are the most recurring example of this trope in fiction. These do exist in Real Life , but are very rare in the wild. Lionesses prefer males with darker mane, and any large ambush predator living in tropical biomes simply cannot camouflage themselves effectively with a white pelt. There are currently only a handful of wild white lions and no wild white tigers (though they did very rarely occur in the past). They are considerably more common in captivity, but as the alleles for white fur are recessive in both lions and tigers, captive examples are often inbred, universally so in the case of white tigers, which leads to loads of physical defects and health issues, hence why conservationists and zoologists very much do not approve of this practice, since it's ethically dubious and serves no purpose in preserving these endangers animals. Outside of these mutations, the closest thing to this trope reality has to offer are snow leopards, who have white-gray fur with darker markings, though they are also the smallest of the extant big cats (compared to both its fellow panthers and largest feline the cougar). Overall, this trope is very much a case of Awesome, but Impractical in the real world. Similarly, the extinct cave lion ( Panthera spelaea ) and the Eurasian scimitar cat ( Homotherium latidens ) are sometimes portrayed this way in paleontological media due to living in the frigid mammoth steppe environment , but those were subjected to Science Marches On once we discovered four mummified cave lion cubs and one scimitar cub in the Siberian permafrost, which show that cave lions were similar in color to extant lions while H. latidens seems to have had a dark brown pelt with a possible lighter underside (which is common in cats). note It's worth noting that the mammoth steppe was a uniquely dry and windswept environment where snow actually had trouble building up, so there might not have been any selective pressure for camouflage against the snow. The notion that they might have had a "winter coat" (where they turn white during winter) also isn't terribly reasonable given how no extant felids living in colder climes, be they big (Siberian tigers, Amur leopards, snow leopards, cougars), mid-sized (lynx) or small (like the Pallas and Andean cat), show radical changes in coat color with the seasons, nor does any land predator bigger than an Arctic fox for that matter (Arctic wolves, for example, stay white all year round). If the writers really didn't do their research, the famous Smilodon might be portrayed this way and inaccurately be shown inhabiting snowy environments , even though it almost certainly didn't look this way, since it mainly inhabited temperate to tropical environments. This trope can also apply to characters who use a white panther Animal Motif . The Great White Feline can be seen as a symbol of power, uniqueness, mysticism, magic, authority etc. Given the frequency to associate such creatures in media with the supernatural, this trope could be seen as the big cat equivalent of Mystical White Hair . Expect this trope to come up in the form of the White Tiger of the West when The Four Gods of Chinese Mythology are featured in a work. Generally this trope applies to heroic characters, but rare villainous examples do exist , perhaps overlapping with Albinos Are Freaks . Sister Trope to White Wolves Are Special and White Stallion . White Bunny also involves a white animal, but has innocent connotations instead. Note: Mega Neko with white fur do not qualify as this. This trope applies to large non-domestic cats with white fur. A Mega Neko is a giant domestic cat (or other cat species that is normally small in size).
